abstract: Since the inception of the Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) initiative in Afghanistan, members of the nongovernmental organization (NGO) community have voiced significant concerns, arguing that the integration of military and humanitarian operations creates "blurred lines" that prevent insurgents from distinguishing between combatants and aid workers. Many organizations have hypothesized that this overlap directly increases the likelihood of attacks on humanitarian personnel. Despite the prevalence of this claim in policy circles and media outlets, there remains a notable lack of empirical evidence to validate it. This study addresses this gap by employing a panel-corrected standard error regression model to analyze data across all 34 Afghan provinces during 2010 and 2011. While preliminary results indicate that NGOs reported a higher frequency of security incidents in provinces containing PRTs, a more nuanced analysis reveals that this correlation was exclusive to provinces where teams were led by non-U.S. forces. These findings challenge the universality of the "blurred lines" theory as a comprehensive explanation for the deterioration of aid worker security.
